does: This paper establishes a theoretical framework for Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) by analyzing convergence properties of reward learning under different comparison models. The authors prove that maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) converges when the true reward is linear under both Bradley-Terry-Luce and Plackett-Luce models. Critically, they show that while MLE converges for reward estimation, it can fail for policy optimization, whereas pessimistic MLE yields improved policies under coverage assumptions. The work also demonstrates that under the Plackett-Luce model, using true MLE is asymptotically more efficient than decomposing k-wise comparisons into pairwise comparisons, while both approaches converge. These results provide the first formal convergence guarantees validating empirical RLHF practices.
connects: This work extends classical preference learning frameworks—Bradley-Terry-Luce and Plackett-Luce models—to the reinforcement learning setting where learned reward models must actually drive policy optimization. It builds on prior work in inverse reinforcement learning and preference-based learning, unifying RLHF with max-entropy IRL theory. The paper responds to the empirical success of RLHF methods in large language models like InstructGPT, which lacked theoretical justification. By addressing the gap between reward convergence and policy performance, it extends pessimism principles from offline RL into the human feedback setting. The analysis also connects to sample complexity bounds in learning theory, establishing foundations missing from previous empirical RLHF work.
enables: This theoretical foundation enables more principled algorithm design for RLHF systems by clarifying when and why existing methods work or fail. The characterization of pessimistic MLE performance motivates robust reward learning approaches for practical deployment. The first sample complexity bounds for max-entropy IRL open avenues for understanding data efficiency in preference-based learning. The unification of RLHF and IRL suggests cross-pollination between these communities on both theory and methods. The analysis of comparison model choice—pairwise versus k-wise—informs practical decisions about human feedback collection. Future work can build on these foundations to develop convergence-guaranteed algorithms for nonlinear rewards, handle distribution shift in policy learning, and extend to more complex preference structures beyond pairwise comparisons.
